sources.name: Táin Bó Cúailnge (The Cattle Raid of Cooley)
sources.description: A central text in the Ulster Cycle of Irish mythology, featuring Fergus mac Róich and descriptions of Caladbolg's power.
sources.title: Táin Bó Cúailnge (The Cattle Raid of Cooley)

sources.name: Cath Maige Tuired (The Battle of Mag Tuired)
sources.description: A key text detailing the battles of the Tuatha Dé Danann, providing context for the mythical smiths and the potential use of magical weapons.
sources.title: Cath Maige Tuired (The Battle of Mag Tuired)

sources.name: Annals of the Four Masters
sources.description: A chronicle of Irish history, although later than the early myths, it provides a valuable overview of traditional narratives and characters.
sources.title: Annals of the Four Masters

sources.name: Dictionary of Celtic Mythology by Peter Berresford Ellis
sources.description: A comprehensive secondary source offering insights into Celtic mythology and its key figures and objects.
sources.title: Dictionary of Celtic Mythology by Peter Berresford Ellis

sources.name: Early Irish Myths and Sagas (Penguin Classics)
sources.description: A collection of translated Irish texts, offering accessible versions of the primary sources.
sources.title: Early Irish Myths and Sagas (Penguin Classics)

longDescription:
The Hard-Cleft - Ireland's Rainbow Sword Caladbolg (Old Irish: "Hard Lightning" or "Hard-Cleft," also spelled Caladcholg)
                    was a legendary sword in Irish mythology, wielded by Fergus mac Roich, one of
                    the great heroes of the Ulster Cycle. This massive blade was said to be so
                    powerful that when swung, it appeared as a rainbow arcing across the sky and
                    could slice the tops off hills with a single stroke.
name: Caladbolg
powers: Could slice hilltops, appeared as a rainbow when swung

extendedContent.title: Description and Nature
extendedContent.content:
Caladbolg (Old Irish: "Hard Lightning" or "Hard-Cleft," also spelled Caladcholg)
                    was a legendary sword in Irish mythology, wielded by Fergus mac Roich, one of
                    the great heroes of the Ulster Cycle. This massive blade was said to be so
                    powerful that when swung, it appeared as a rainbow arcing across the sky and
                    could slice the tops off hills with a single stroke.
                
                
                    The name Caladbolg has been linked etymologically to the Welsh Caledfwlch, which
                    in turn may be the origin of Excalibur (through Latin Caliburnus). This potential
                    connection places Caladbolg among the ancestors of the most famous sword in
                    Western mythology, suggesting a common Celtic root for these legendary blades.
                
                
                    Unlike many magical swords that grant invincibility or have specific mystical
                    powers, Caladbolg's fame comes primarily from its sheer destructive force -
                    it was simply the most powerful weapon any mortal could wield, capable of
                    devastating feats that reshaped the landscape itself.

Fergus mac Roich was one of the greatest warriors of the Ulster Cycle, a former
                    king of Ulster who went into exile after King Conchobar mac Nessa betrayed the
                    sons of Usnech (including Naoise, lover of Deirdre) who had been under Fergus's
                    protection. This betrayal drove Fergus to join Ulster's enemies, the Connacht
                    army of Queen Medb.
                
                
                    Despite fighting against his former homeland, Fergus remained bound by certain
                    geasa (sacred obligations) and complex loyalties. His relationship with his
                    foster-son Cu Chulainn, Ulster's greatest hero, created constant tension during
                    the Tain Bo Cuailnge (The Cattle Raid of Cooley), the central epic of the Ulster
                    Cycle.
                

                Caladbolg in the Tain Bo Cuailnge
                
                    During the great cattle raid, Fergus repeatedly entered battle with Caladbolg,
                    but Queen Medb or circumstances often prevented him from using its full power
                    against the Ulstermen - particularly Cu Chulainn. In their climactic duel,
                    Fergus agreed to yield to Cu Chulainn in exchange for a future favor, rather
                    than use Caladbolg to kill his beloved foster-son.
extendedContent.title: Fergus mac Roich

extendedContent.content:
Linguistic scholars have traced a likely evolution from Irish Caladbolg through
                    Welsh Caledfwlch to the Latin Caliburnus used by Geoffrey of Monmouth, and
                    finally to the French Excalibur. This connection suggests that Arthur's famous
                    sword may have Celtic mythological roots.
                
                
                    The similarities extend beyond etymology:
                
                
                    Both are swords of exceptional power, beyond ordinary mortal weapons
                    Both are associated with rightful kingship (Fergus was a former king;
                    Excalibur proves Arthur's right to rule)
                    Both appear with supernatural light effects
                    Both represent the peak of martial achievement in their respective traditions
                
                
                    Whether Excalibur directly descends from Caladbolg or both share a common
                    Celtic ancestor, the connection highlights the deep mythological heritage
                    underlying Arthurian legend.
extendedContent.title: Connection to Excalibur

keyMyths.title: The Forging of Caladbolg
keyMyths.description: While details of its creation are scarce, Caladbolg is often attributed to the mythical smiths of the Tuatha Dé Danann, beings of immense power and skill. Its origins are shrouded in mystery, hinting at a forging process involving potent magic and otherworldly materials.

keyMyths.title: Fergus mac Róich's Wielding of Caladbolg
keyMyths.description: Fergus mac Róich, a renowned Ulster warrior, is the most famous wielder of Caladbolg. The sword is described as being incredibly large and powerful, necessitating him to use both hands to swing it effectively, cleaving hills in two.
keyMyths.source: Táin Bó Cúailnge (The Cattle Raid of Cooley)

keyMyths.title: The Sword's Thirst for Blood
keyMyths.description: Caladbolg is depicted as possessing an insatiable thirst for blood, requiring constant use in battle. When swung, it could reportedly mow down entire ranks of enemies, leaving a trail of carnage in its wake and creating a rainbow-like arc in the sky.
keyMyths.source: Táin Bó Cúailnge (The Cattle Raid of Cooley)

keyMyths.title: Caladbolg and the Battle of Mag Tuired
keyMyths.description: Although not explicitly detailed, it's implied that Caladbolg played a role in the conflicts between the Tuatha Dé Danann and their rivals, particularly the Fomorians. Its immense power would have been a valuable asset in these mythical battles for control of Ireland.
keyMyths.source: Cath Maige Tuired (The Battle of Mag Tuired)

keyMyths.title: The Loss or Disappearance of Caladbolg
keyMyths.description: The ultimate fate of Caladbolg is ambiguous; it is not explicitly stated how Fergus lost the sword or what became of it after his death. This ambiguity adds to the sword's mystique, leaving room for speculation about its continued existence in the mythical realm.
